next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The paddleboards were still dripping under the bat lab when Beto came up the screened walk with the camp phone in one hand and a piece of toast in the other, chewing like whatever panic was in the receiver had interrupted breakfast rather than replaced it. He had blue pen across his knuckles again, torque numbers from the outboard manual he kept trying to memorize between dish duty and dock work, and one of the digits had smeared where he had wiped butter away with the heel of his hand.

"Imogen wants you and Dr. Acosta at the county dock," he said, voice full around the last bite. "Right now. She said right now twice, so I think she means it the bad way."

Rhea came out of the lab behind Mina with her braid half redone and a fresh alcohol wipe smell clinging to her wrists. They had been packing last night's bat cards into labeled sleeves, quiet work after the paddleboard survey, letting the tenderness of the evening keep its own shape without naming it too much. Mina still had black mangrove grit under one thumbnail and the ache in her shoulders from poling through root shade. For one drowsy hour she had believed the day might stay small.

"What happened?" Rhea asked.

Beto shrugged one shoulder. The oversized camp T-shirt slid down and showed the pale burn ladder on his wrist from the kitchen fryer back in May. "County radio. Something about positives. Something about a board member's nephew. She was already swearing before she hung up."

That narrowed it enough.

The walk to the mosquito dock took them through shell glare and wet heat so dense it seemed to...
open_hooks_to_carry: Rhea and Mina survey bats by paddle instead of motor, changing the chapter's tempo and scale.
Rhea names the difference between desire, rescue, and witness.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...pter inside her. Not because she no longer wanted him. Because he had named the role she had kept trying to half-deny and half-upgrade into love. Path. Route. Access. He had been a man and a boat and a body and a joke in the kitchen and a hand on the back of her neck in the dark. He had also been the path. All the wanting in the world did not change the order in which she had learned to need him.

She stepped out into the wet walkway with the dry bag over one shoulder and the folded sheet tucked under her arm. Rain blew through the screen in a fine mist and freckled her forearms. In the dark below, water moved around the pilings with that thick black sound the channels made when the tide was turning somewhere you could not see. The camp's lights glowed weakly on the runoff. From the rookery came the late, irritated squall of birds settling badly.

Her own room felt narrower than it had that morning, the camp mattress humped in the center, the air stale from being shut during rain. She set the dry bag on the bunk and took the annex badge from her pocket. The plastic was tacky with humidity. Her younger face smiled up through mildew bloom and scratch marks, still certain that proximity to wonder counted as possession.

Outside, the storm drains gulped and overflowed and tried again. Every structure on the stilts spoke its burden aloud in weather. Mina laid the badge on top of the folded sheet and understood, with a clarity so sharp it was almost restful, that there was no corner of the story left now that had not been touched by labor, appetite, or somebody's bill coming due.
state_deltas_required: The triangle's fracture, motor access loss, and the need to decide what kind of witness Rhea and Mina can be together
Rhea chooses Mina as a moral confidante but refuses the fantasy that Mina can replace her life.
